title: Terce: The Silent Lamb
subtitle: Christ stands mute before Pilate, then is scourged and crowned with thorns

stillness: Come now into the third hour, mournful and sad.
Like a lamb before its shearer, He fell silent.
Be still, and let Him not open His mouth in you either.
gospel: They led the Lord back to Pilate.
And the dogs, rejoicing in the pact of Herod and Pilate,
pressed their accusations with great boldness.
Yet neither Pilate nor Herod, questioning Him closely,
could find in Him any cause of death.
When He was accused by the chief priests and the elders, He answered nothing.
Then Pilate said to Him: Do you not hear how many things they testify against you?
And He answered him not a word,
so that the governor wondered exceedingly.
Now at the feast the governor was wont to release one prisoner, whom the people would.
He had then a notorious prisoner, called Barabbas.
Pilate said: Whom will you that I release to you: Barabbas, or Jesus who is called Christ?
And as he sat on the judgment seat, his wife sent to him, saying:
Have nothing to do with that just man,
for I have suffered many things this day in a dream because of Him.
behold: And so, as Anselm says, look attentively how He stands before Pilate the governor:
His head bowed, His eyes cast down,
His face calm, His speech rare,
ready for insults, prompt for the lashes.
Behold the Judge of all, standing before a worthless and unworthy judge,
meek and silent, accused, answering nothing.
Sometimes the Lord answers, sometimes He answers not.
Where He answers, like a good shepherd He teaches, and fights for the sheep entrusted to Him;
where He answers not, like a lamb to be slain for the whole flock He keeps silence, and offers up His patience.
Now behold: stripped, and bound to the column.
Who was so bold as to strip you? Who so reckless as to bind you to the pillar?
Who so sacrilegious as to scourge you?
They took the reed from His hand and struck His sacred head,
driving the thorns deeper in, that the spikes might pierce more fiercely.
For vile persons are wont to be beaten with sticks, like beasts.
consider: Pilate himself confesses that he found nothing in Christ worthy of death;
yet whom he absolved by judgment, he crucified by his office.
Hear, O blind Jew; hear, O cruel pagan.
Accused, the Lord is silent, for He has no need of defence.
He does not confirm the charge by His silence, but despises it by not refuting it.
They are eager to be defended who fear to be conquered.
Better is the cause that is not defended, and yet is proved.
Susanna kept silent, and conquered.
He kept silence justly, for through their false accusations they were unworthy to hear Him.
He kept silence mercifully, lest from His answer they should grow worse.
He kept silence by design, lest, excusing Himself, He be released, and the profit of the cross — our salvation, our redemption — be deferred.
He kept silence as an example, that He might give us a pattern of patience.
The devil, now understanding that through Christ he would lose his spoils,
repented that he had caused Him to be seized,
and sent dreams to this woman, that through her the death of Christ might be hindered.
As once through a woman he had brought death into the world,
so now he strives to free Christ through a woman,
lest by the death of Christ he lose the empire of death.
But already, by the will and power of God, it was decreed otherwise.
O Sun of justice, you withdrew your rays,
and therefore there is darkness, and the powers of darkness;
the lashes of eternal affliction were owed to us —
you willed to be scourged for us, that you might so mercifully snatch us from them.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the third hour of the day willed to be scourged for us,
free me from the scourges of that eternal wrath which I have deserved;
by the piercings of your most sacred head, pierce my mind,
that I may have strength to shun the delights that harm me,
and grant me, after penance, to attain to the crown.
You who, mocked in so many ways, were at last sentenced and condemned to death,
make me escape the devil's mockery and eternal death.
You bore your cross, O Lord: grant me to take up my cross and follow you —
my own cross, the one owed to my sins,
and my own cross willingly and devoutly borne,
that through it I may come to glory. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Sicut agnus coram tondente se obmutescet, et non aperiet os suum.
latinJewel.english: Like a lamb before its shearer, He shall be dumb, and shall not open His mouth.
